title: AMS encodes security as a subscriber pattern, generalizing D0010
content: ams://canon/principles/security-as-subscriber-pattern committed as a Tier-2 principle. Generalizes D0010 (observability via subscriber, not wire) to the security class: signing, audit, policy enforcement, and anomaly detection each operate as polymorphic subscribers with declared roles, never as wire features. Each sub-pattern documents the application of the polymorphic-subscriber model to one specific security function. The class is recommended convention; new security roles can be added by declaration without protocol change. The principle creates the structural foundation for any future threat-model work the project takes on, including the open per-conversation runtime isolation question carried in 2026-05-02-ams-per-conversation-runtime-isolation-idea.tsv.

title: The inversion principle is promoted upstream to klappy.dev as klappy://canon/principles/doing-less-enables-more
content: klappy://canon/principles/doing-less-enables-more committed in a separate repo (klappy/klappy.dev). The principle is the empirical claim behind why vodka-architecture wins where opinionated stacks lose: substrates win by refusing to own layers above them. Lineage: TCP/IP, Unix pipes, HTTP, oddkit, AMS — each was contemporaneously criticized as too thin and turned out to be exactly thin enough. The principle sits as a complement to canon/principles/vodka-architecture.md (the discipline) by documenting why the discipline pays. Tier 2. Two ams://canon/principles already in place serve as downstream applications: per-query-dynamic-orchestration (one specific instance) and envelope-altitude-consensus (another specific instance). The upstream-vs-overlay split follows the established pattern in this canon: general principles live at klappy://, AMS-specific applications live at ams://.

title: Security claims at the wire layer are forbidden by the security-as-subscriber-pattern principle
content: The bounded-power section of ams://canon/principles/security-as-subscriber-pattern names what security subscribers can do (observe, sign, attest, alert, request termination by convention) and what they cannot do (block tokens, prevent joining, force termination, gatekeep at the wire). Future proposals to add wire-level security enforcement — moderation primitives in PROTOCOL.md, mandatory signing on the wire, capability-token validation at the broker — fail this principle's bounded-power test. The wire structurally cannot enforce security; that is the property that lets every kind of security live above it. Per Axiom 2 (A Claim Is a Debt), wire-level security claims also create a verifiability debt the substrate cannot honestly pay. This constraint is the gate for any future canon work in the security area.

title: Cross-repo principle promotion follows the same overlay/upstream pattern AMS already uses
content: The decision to put the inversion principle upstream at klappy://canon/principles/doing-less-enables-more rather than at ams://canon/principles/some-local-name follows the same overlay/upstream pattern established by canon/knowledge-base.md and exemplified by ams://canon/principles/vodka-architecture-applied (which complements upstream klappy://canon/principles/vodka-architecture). The pattern: when a principle generalizes beyond one program (oddkit, AMS, future MCP servers), it lives upstream once and is referenced from each program-specific overlay. The pattern produces a working canon graph rather than duplicated principles per repo. Forward implication: future cross-repo principle promotions go through the same shape — write once at klappy://, reference from any ams:// applied principle that needs it. The DRY-canon-says-it-once rule generalizes to cross-repo as well as in-repo duplication.

title: klappy.dev does not appear to use the TSV journal pattern that AMS uses
content: Inspection of the klappy/klappy.dev repository structure shows no journal/ directory and no TSV journal artifacts comparable to the agent-messaging-service overlay's encoding pattern. The DOLCHE+H journal convention (one TSV per encoding session, committed to journal/) is currently AMS-overlay-specific. klappy.dev appears to record decisions and changes through canon document evolution and (presumably) git history alone. Open question for future canon hygiene: whether the TSV journal pattern should be promoted upstream as a klappy://-shaped convention applicable to any oddkit-served knowledge base, or whether it should remain an AMS overlay choice that other overlays may or may not adopt. No action this turn; flagging for the next session that touches journal conventions or oddkit governance.

title: Both files require PRs to their respective repositories
content: Two PRs need to be opened by the operator (Claude does not have push permissions in this session and the PAT in project instructions is scoped to klappy/klappy.dev + klappy/oddkit, not klappy/agent-messaging-service): (1) PR to klappy/agent-messaging-service containing all canon and journal artifacts produced this session — three principles (envelope-altitude-consensus.md new, operator-as-subscriber.md modified, security-as-subscriber-pattern.md new), eight resonance pages (the entire Tier-1 batch plus the Tier-2 batch), and five journal TSV entries; (2) PR to klappy/klappy.dev containing one new file: canon/principles/doing-less-enables-more.md. The two PRs are independent; either can land first. Suggested branch names, commit messages, and PR descriptions are provided in the next-turn session output for direct copy-paste.
